items.q: What compliance frameworks do you support?
items.a: NIST CSF 2.0, ISO 27001, SOC 2, HIPAA, PCI-DSS, GDPR, and CMMC. Our cybersecurity programs are tailored to your regulatory footprint and built for audit-readiness — not just checkbox compliance.

items.q: Do you offer fractional CISO services?
items.a: Yes. Our Fractional CISO program gives you direct access to James Gorman and our senior security team at a fraction of the cost of a full-time hire — with the depth and continuity of a long-term partner.

items.q: How do you approach multi-cloud security?
items.a: We build cloud-agnostic security architectures and governance models that protect data and applications across AWS, Azure, GCP, and hybrid environments. No vendor lock-in. No security gaps at the seams.

items.q: Who leads your cybersecurity engagements?
items.a: Every engagement is led by senior cybersecurity practitioners — not junior analysts. James Gorman, our CISO, brings 30+ years of experience across federal, healthcare, and financial services. Senior expertise, start to finish.

items.q: How quickly can you respond to an active incident?
items.a: Our incident response team is available 24/7 for active engagements. For organizations with a retainer in place, initial triage begins within 2 hours. For new engagements, we typically mobilize within 24–48 hours depending on severity.

items.q: What is the typical engagement length for a cybersecurity program?
items.a: Initial assessments run 2–4 weeks. Full program implementations range from 3–12 months depending on scope and your current maturity level. Fractional CISO retainers are ongoing and reviewed quarterly.

data-consulting.heading: Data Consulting Questions
data-consulting.subheading: Clarity on how we approach data strategy and implementation.

items.q: Where do most organizations go wrong with their data strategy?
items.a: The most common failure is treating data as an IT problem instead of a business asset. Organizations invest in tools before establishing governance, launch analytics initiatives without clean foundations, and build data products nobody uses because they weren't tied to business decisions. We solve this by starting with business outcomes, not technology.

items.q: What does a data governance program actually look like in practice?
items.a: It starts with defining data ownership, classification, and quality standards across your organization. We establish policies, stewardship roles, and audit trails — then implement the tooling and workflows to enforce them. The outcome is trusted data that your teams actually rely on for decisions.

items.q: How do you handle AI readiness before we have clean data?
items.a: That is exactly the sequence we work through. AI models are only as good as the data feeding them. We assess your data foundation first — quality, completeness, lineage, governance — then build the architecture needed to support AI workloads. Skipping this step is the primary reason AI pilots fail.

items.q: Do you work with specific cloud data platforms?
items.a: We are platform-agnostic and work across Snowflake, Databricks, AWS Redshift, Azure Synapse, Google BigQuery, and modern data lakehouse architectures. Our recommendation is always based on your workload economics and existing stack — not vendor relationships.

items.q: How long does a data strategy engagement typically take?
items.a: An initial data strategy assessment and roadmap runs 4–8 weeks. Implementation phases are scoped in 60–90 day sprints with clear deliverables. Most organizations see measurable improvement in data quality and decision-making within the first 90 days.

items.q: Can you work alongside our existing data team?
items.a: Yes — and that is often the best model. We embed with your existing team, upskill where needed, and build programs your internal resources can own and maintain long-term. We do not create dependency.

digital-engineering.heading: Engineering Questions
digital-engineering.subheading: How we build, migrate, and modernize technology at scale.

items.q: How do you approach legacy system modernization without disrupting operations?
items.a: We use a strangler fig approach — incrementally replacing legacy components while keeping existing systems running. This reduces risk, allows rollback at each phase, and means your business never stops during transformation. We design migration paths with zero-downtime deployments built in from the start.

items.q: What cloud platforms do you specialize in?
items.a: AWS, Azure, and GCP — with deep expertise in all three. We also specialize in multi-cloud architectures for organizations that need portability and resilience across providers. Our cloud recommendations are based on workload fit and total cost of ownership, not certifications.

items.q: How do you handle DevSecOps integration in existing engineering teams?
items.a: We assess your current CI/CD pipeline, security posture, and deployment practices. Then we embed security tooling, policy-as-code controls, and automated testing directly into your delivery pipeline — so security is an engineering practice, not a gate at the end.

items.q: Do you build products or only advise?
items.a: Both. We design architecture and own delivery. Our engineering teams build cloud-native applications, APIs, and data pipelines. We stay through deployment and stabilization — not just the strategy phase. You get a working product, not a roadmap slide.

items.q: How do you price engineering engagements?
items.a: We offer fixed-fee project engagements for well-scoped work and retainer-based models for ongoing engineering support. We do not use time-and-materials billing that creates incentives to extend projects. Pricing is tied to outcomes and milestones.

items.q: What is your approach to Agentic AI in engineering workflows?
items.a: We assess your data foundation and architecture readiness before recommending agentic AI implementations. From there, we design agent workflows that integrate with your existing systems — automating high-value repetitive tasks and accelerating development cycles without introducing governance gaps.

sales-improvement.heading: Sales Improvement Questions
sales-improvement.subheading: How we fix the story, the process, and the results.

items.q: What is the most common reason technology sales teams underperform?
items.a: The story. Most technology organizations build their sales narrative around features, certifications, and capabilities — not the business problems their buyers are trying to solve. Your bottom 50% of reps cannot articulate business value. That is a messaging problem, not a talent problem.

items.q: How does the 101/202/303 methodology work in practice?
items.a: 101 establishes context — understanding the buyer's world before pitching anything. 202 surfaces the real tension — what is actually blocking their goals, not just the symptoms. 303 paints the future state where they are the hero and your firm is the guide. The deal becomes the natural next step, not a close.

items.q: Do you work with sales teams directly or only at the leadership level?
items.a: Both. We design the narrative and framework at the executive level, then train and coach individual reps and account executives on delivery. The methodology only works if it reaches the people in front of buyers every day.

items.q: How do you measure success on a sales improvement engagement?
items.a: We align on 3–5 specific metrics before the engagement starts — typically win rate, average deal size, sales cycle length, and rep ramp time. We report against these metrics throughout the engagement and build a 90-day success plan tied directly to pipeline outcomes.

items.q: Can you help with CRM strategy and revenue operations?
items.a: Yes. Clean revenue operations is a prerequisite for sales improvement. We assess your CRM setup, pipeline hygiene, forecasting accuracy, and reporting infrastructure. The best story in the world falls apart without the operational backbone to support consistent execution.

items.q: How long before we see results?
items.a: Most teams see measurable improvements in pipeline quality and conversation effectiveness within 30–60 days of completing narrative training. Full revenue impact typically shows in the 60–90 day window as new opportunities progress through the pipeline with the new approach.

ai-agents.heading: AI Agents for Business
ai-agents.subheading: Practical answers to the questions executives are actually asking.

items.q: What is the difference between AI agents and the AI tools we already use?
items.a: Most AI tools respond to prompts — they do one thing when asked. AI agents can plan multi-step tasks, use external tools, make decisions, and take action autonomously within defined guardrails. The difference is the shift from AI as a calculator to AI as a teammate.

items.q: What do we need in place before deploying AI agents?
items.a: Three things: trustworthy data, clear governance, and defined use cases with measurable ROI. Organizations that deploy agents on top of fragmented data or without access controls create more risk than value. Our AI Readiness Diagnostic identifies exactly where your gaps are before you spend on tooling.

items.q: How do you ensure AI agents do not make unauthorized or risky decisions?
items.a: We design agents with layered guardrails — defined action boundaries, human-in-the-loop checkpoints for high-stakes decisions, audit logging, and rollback capabilities. Governance is not an afterthought in our agent architecture. It is the foundation.

items.q: What business processes are the best candidates for agentic AI?
items.a: Processes that are high-frequency, rules-based, data-intensive, and currently requiring human coordination across systems are the best starting points. Common high-ROI candidates include compliance monitoring, data quality workflows, lead qualification, reporting generation, and IT operations automation.

items.q: How do we measure ROI on an AI agents implementation?
items.a: We baseline current process metrics before implementation — time per task, error rate, headcount involved, and cost per transaction. Post-deployment, we track the delta. Most well-scoped agent implementations show positive ROI within 90 days on targeted workflows.

items.q: Will AI agents replace our employees?
items.a: That is the wrong frame. The organizations winning with AI are redirecting their people to higher-value work — not reducing headcount. Agents handle the repetitive, coordination-heavy tasks. Your people handle judgment, relationships, and strategy. Done right, it makes your existing team significantly more effective.

calculators.heading: Calculator Questions
calculators.subheading: How we build these estimates and what they are based on.

items.q: How accurate are these estimates?
items.a: These calculators use publicly validated data from IBM Cost of a Data Breach, Verizon DBIR, Coveware, Ponemon Institute, Gartner, and Flexera. They are designed to give leadership teams a credible order-of-magnitude estimate — not a precise audit figure. Actual costs vary based on factors specific to your environment, response speed, and insurance coverage.

items.q: What data sources are used in the cybersecurity incident calculator?
items.a: FBI IC3 annual reports for BEC statistics, Coveware quarterly ransomware reports for ransom demand and recovery data, IBM Cost of a Data Breach 2024 for industry multipliers and average response costs, Verizon DBIR for breach pattern analysis, and HHS/OCR published fine data for HIPAA compliance calculations.

items.q: Can we use these estimates in a board presentation or business case?
items.a: Yes — with appropriate framing. These are estimates based on industry averages, not organization-specific analysis. We recommend citing the underlying data sources and noting that your actual exposure may be higher or lower. For a board-ready risk quantification, our Technical Risk Assessment delivers organization-specific findings.

items.q: How is the Cost of Inaction calculated?
items.a: It combines three components: productivity loss from current gaps (based on McKinsey research on technology program efficiency gains), risk accumulation rate (IBM breach likelihood data applied to security delay), and competitive gap cost (Gartner market share loss estimates for delayed digital transformation). Each is calculated monthly and multiplied by your delay period.

items.q: Why does the cloud waste percentage vary so much?
items.a: Cloud waste is highly dependent on your FinOps maturity, Reserved Instance coverage, and environment hygiene. Gartner's 32% industry average is a reasonable baseline, but we have seen organizations with no FinOps practice wasting 45%+ and mature programs at under 10%. The calculator adjusts based on the factors you select.
